buddhas_of_the_fortunate_eon.name: Krakucchanda
buddhas_of_the_fortunate_eon.qualities: The first Buddha of the Bhadrakalpa. Overcame the afflictions of the early age.

buddhas_of_the_fortunate_eon.name: Kanakamuni
buddhas_of_the_fortunate_eon.qualities: The second Buddha. Golden sage who taught the perfection of giving.

buddhas_of_the_fortunate_eon.name: Kasyapa
buddhas_of_the_fortunate_eon.qualities: The third Buddha. Taught pure discipline and luminous awareness.

buddhas_of_the_fortunate_eon.name: Shakyamuni
buddhas_of_the_fortunate_eon.qualities: The fourth Buddha. The historical Buddha who taught the Middle Way and the Four Noble Truths.

buddhas_of_the_fortunate_eon.name: Maitreya
buddhas_of_the_fortunate_eon.qualities: The fifth and future Buddha. The embodiment of loving-kindness (Maitri).

arya_sanghata_sutra.description: A deeply transformative Mahayana sutra. Hearing it cleanses eons of negative karma and aligns the listener with the Bodhisattva path.
themes: The nature of arising and passing

sutra_of_golden_light.description: A sutra focused on the protection of nations, the confession of faults, and the profound nature of emptiness.

key_figures.name: Sarasvati
key_figures.role: Goddess of eloquence, memory, and wisdom.

key_figures.name: Sri (Lakshmi)
key_figures.role: Goddess of wealth, prosperity, and merit.

key_figures.name: Drdha
key_figures.role: Earth Goddess who sustains the land.

key_figures.name: The Four Heavenly Kings
key_figures.role: Protectors of the dharma in the four directions.

yidams_and_mantras.name: Chenrezig (Avalokiteshvara)
yidams_and_mantras.purpose: To awaken compassion and heal the heart.

yidams_and_mantras.name: Green Tara
yidams_and_mantras.description: The mother of all Buddhas, swiftly acting to remove fear and obstacles.
yidams_and_mantras.purpose: Victory from difficulty, swift protection, overcoming fear.

yidams_and_mantras.name: Medicine Buddha (Bhaisajyaguru)
yidams_and_mantras.description: The supreme healer whose lapis lazuli light cures all physical and mental illnesses.
yidams_and_mantras.dharani_mantra: Tayata Om Bekandze Bekandze Maha Bekandze Radza Samudgate Soha

yidams_and_mantras.name: Vajrasattva
yidams_and_mantras.description: The principle of purity, used for clearing karmic obscurations.
yidams_and_mantras.dharani_mantra: Om Vajrasattva Samaya Manu Palaya... (100 Syllable Mantra)

yidams_and_mantras.name: Cundi Buddha Mother (准提佛母 / Chunti)
yidams_and_mantras.description: The Buddha Mother of seven million Buddhas, one of the most powerful esoteric Buddhist deities for purification and wish-fulfillment. Manifests with a white or golden-yellow body, three eyes, and eighteen arms each holding a Dharma implement, seated upon a white lotus. The eighteen arms symbolize the eighteen distinctive qualities (āveṇika-dharmāḥ) of a Buddha. A uniquely accessible practice — Cundi's dharani does NOT require abhisheka (empowerment) and may be practiced by anyone.
yidams_and_mantras.dharani_mantra: Namaḥ Saptānāṃ Samyaksaṃbuddha Koṭīnāṃ Tadyathā Oṃ Cale Cule Cuṇḍe Svāhā (heart: Oṃ Cale Cule Cuṇḍe Svāhā)
yidams_and_mantras.purpose: Purification of all karmic obscurations, wish-fulfillment for beneficial goals, removal of obstacles to spiritual practice.
yidams_and_mantras.color: white or yellow (golden) — varies by tradition
yidams_and_mantras.mudra: Cundi Mudra (准提印) — fingers interlocked with both index fingers pointing upward, forming a pointed peak

iconography.arms_meaning: The 18 distinctive qualities of a Buddha (āveṇika-dharmāḥ)
iconography.implements: Each of the 18 hands holds a different Dharma implement (vajra, wheel, lotus, sword, mala, bow, arrow, conch, vase, etc.)

yidams_and_mantras.name: Manjushri
yidams_and_mantras.dharani_mantra: Om Ah Ra Pa Tsa Na Dhi
yidams_and_mantras.purpose: Cutting through delusion, gaining profound insight.

vajrayana_foundational_entities.name: Medicine Buddha (Bhaiṣajyaguru Vaiḍūryaprabhārāja)

iconography.body_color: Transparent lapis lazuli blue, radiating healing light
iconography.right_hand: Mudrā of supreme giving over the right knee, holding a myrobalan (arura) plant — the king of medicinal herbs
iconography.left_hand: Meditation mudrā in the lap, holding a lapis lazuli begging bowl filled with healing nectar
iconography.posture: Seated in vajra posture (full lotus) on a lotus and moon disc
iconography.robes: Three dharma robes of a fully ordained monk

attributes: Lapis begging bowl of healing nectar
vajrayana_foundational_entities.pure_land: Eastern Pure Land of Pure Lapis Lazuli (Vaiḍūrya)
vajrayana_foundational_entities.vows: 12 great vows to heal physical illness, mental afflictions, and spiritual obscurations of all beings
vajrayana_foundational_entities.purpose: Healing of physical and mental illness; alleviation of suffering; restoration of balance and vitality

vajrayana_foundational_entities.name: Vajrasattva (Vajrasattva — Diamond Being)

iconography.body_color: Brilliant pure white, translucent and luminous like crystal or snow mountain
iconography.right_hand: Holds a five-pronged vajra (diamond scepter) at the heart — method and compassion
iconography.left_hand: Holds a bell (ghaṇṭā) at the hip — wisdom and emptiness
iconography.posture: Seated in vajra posture, sometimes in union with consort Vajragarvī (Dorje Nyéma)
iconography.ornaments: Wears the thirteen sambhogakāya ornaments (crown, earrings, necklace, bracelets, etc.)
iconography.consort: Vajragarvī (Dorje Nyéma) — when in union, represents the nonduality of bliss and emptiness

vajrayana_foundational_entities.pure_land: Primordial pure land of the dharmadhātu — the Akanistha realm
vajrayana_foundational_entities.vows: Vajrasattva is the condensation of the purity of all Buddhas; whoever recites his mantra is freed from all negative karma
vajrayana_foundational_entities.purpose: Purification of all negative karma and obscurations; restoration of vows; primordial purity of mind

vajrayana_foundational_entities.name: Amitabha Buddha (Amitābha — Infinite Light)

iconography.body_color: Radiant red, the color of the setting sun, glowing with infinite light in all directions
iconography.hands: Both hands rest in the lap in dhyāna (meditation) mudrā, holding a monk's begging bowl (patra) filled with the nectar of immortality
iconography.posture: Seated in vajra posture on a lotus and moon disc
iconography.robes: Three dharma robes of a fully ordained monk
iconography.retinue: Flanked by Avalokiteśvara (right, holding a lotus) and Mahāsthāmaprāpta (left, holding a vase of light) — the Three Holy Ones of the Western Pure Land

vajrayana_foundational_entities.pure_land: Sukhāvatī — the Western Realm of Great Bliss (Pure Land)
vajrayana_foundational_entities.vows: 48 great vows, especially the 18th vow: 'If any being, with sincere faith and aspiration, recites my name even ten times, and is not born in my Pure Land, may I not attain supreme awakening.'
vajrayana_foundational_entities.purpose: Rebirth in Sukhāvatī (Pure Land); peace; transition support for the deceased; transformation of desire into discriminating wisdom

vajrayana_foundational_entities.name: Avalokiteśvara (Thousand-Armed, Thousand-Eyed)
vajrayana_foundational_entities.name_chinese: 千手千眼觀世音菩薩 (Qiānshǒu Qiānyǎn Guānshìyīn Púsà)

iconography.body_color: Brilliant white, suffused with hues of green and soft pink
iconography.heads: Eleven heads stacked in tiers — the 10 stages of bodhisattva development crowned by the head of Amitabha (his spiritual father)
iconography.arms: One thousand arms radiate outward like a mandala; each palm contains an eye of wisdom — seeing all suffering and reaching out to relieve it
iconography.central_hands: Two principal hands pressed together at the heart in añjali mudrā, holding a wish-fulfilling gem (cintāmaṇi)
iconography.principal_attributes_held: White lotus (purity), crystal vase (healing nectar), willow branch (healing), mala (recitation), bow and arrow (skillful means), wheel (dharma teaching)
iconography.posture: Standing gracefully on a lotus, or seated with one leg extended (ready to spring to the aid of beings)

vajrayana_foundational_entities.pure_land: Potalaka Mountain (the southern wish-fulfilling mountain), and also a regent of Sukhāvatī (Amitabha's Pure Land)
vajrayana_foundational_entities.vows: Vowed: 'Should any being hear my name, see my form, or recite my mantra, may they be freed from all suffering. Should I ever waver in compassion, may my head split into a thousand pieces.' The vow was sealed — his head shattered and was reformed as 11 heads, his arms multiplied to 1000.
vajrayana_foundational_entities.purpose: Great Compassion; healing; protection from all dangers; fulfillment of all virtuous wishes

vajrayana_foundational_entities.name: Prajñāpāramitā (the Perfection of Wisdom — Mother of All Buddhas)

iconography.body_color: Radiant golden yellow, luminous like refined gold or the morning sun
iconography.faces_arms: One face and four arms (most common form)
iconography.first_right_hand: Holds a vajra (some forms) or in dharma-teaching mudrā
iconography.second_right_hand: Holds a golden Prajñāpāramitā text (the Perfection of Wisdom sutra) on a blue lotus
iconography.first_left_hand: Holds a blue utpala lotus supporting the wisdom text
iconography.second_left_hand: In dhyāna (meditation) mudrā in the lap
iconography.posture: Seated in vajra posture on a lotus and moon disc, surrounded by an aura of golden light

attributes: Prajñāpāramitā text/book (the Perfection of Wisdom sutra)
vajrayana_foundational_entities.pure_land: The dharmadhātu expanse — the ultimate pure land of wisdom
vajrayana_foundational_entities.vows: Prajñāpāramitā is the mother who gives birth to all the Buddhas of the three times. She is the realization of emptiness itself, personified.
vajrayana_foundational_entities.purpose: Wisdom; realization of emptiness; cutting through all delusion; the perfection that gives birth to all Buddhas

tara_manifestations.description: Tara (Sanskrit: Tārā; Tibetan: Drolma; Chinese: 度母 Dù Mǔ) is the 'Mother of all Buddhas' — the feminine embodiment of enlightened activity and swift compassion. She arose from the tear of Chenrezig (Avalokiteshvara) out of boundless compassion for suffering beings. The 21 principal emanations cover the full spectrum of enlightened action (peace, increase, magnetizing, subjugating).
tara_manifestations.epithet_universal: Jetsunma (Venerable Lady) — She who ferries beings across samsara
tara_manifestations.lineage_note: Principally transmitted via Suryagupta (Kashmiri) and Atisha (Kadampa) lineages; central to all Tibetan schools, particularly the Kagyu and Nyingma.

primary_emanations.name: Green Tara (绿度母)
primary_emanations.iconography: Green-complexioned youthful goddess; right leg extended (ready to spring into action), left leg folded in meditation; right hand in varada mudra (boon-giving) resting on the right knee, left hand at heart in abhaya mudra (fearlessness) holding the stem of a blue utpala lotus (night-blooming celestial lotus); adorned with silks and jewel ornaments; seated on a moon disc atop a lotus.
attributes: springing forth from the lotus to act swiftly
primary_emanations.role: Swift Liberator — acts instantly to remove obstacles and the eight great fears (lions, elephants, fire, snakes, thieves, water, captivity, demons)
primary_emanations.resonance: Compassion in action — the dynamic, swift feminine aspect of enlightenment
primary_emanations.purpose: Swift protection from fear, obstacles, and danger; awakening of compassionate activity; quickening on the path

primary_emanations.name: White Tara (白度母)
primary_emanations.iconography: White-complexioned radiant goddess; seated in vajra posture (full lotus); both hands in varada mudra at the heart; seven eyes of omniscient vision — two ordinary eyes, one third eye on the forehead, plus one eye in each palm and each sole; adorned with silks and jewels; surrounded by the luminous radiance of the full moon; holds the stem of a white lotus.

mudras.both_hands: varada mudra at heart — bestowing boons of long life and healing

attributes: seven eyes (third eye + palms + soles) — seeing all suffering in all realms
full moon aura of healing radiance
primary_emanations.role: Healer and Bestower of Longevity — counterpart to Amitayus in the long-life trinity
primary_emanations.resonance: Full moon energy — luminous, cooling, healing, life-extending
primary_emanations.purpose: Longevity, healing of body and mind, restoration of vitality, wish-fulfilling, purification of illness-causing karma
primary_emanations.companions: Practiced as part of the three long-life deities alongside Amitayus and Ushnishavijaya

emanation_groups.peaceful: Pacifying illness, negative karma, demonic disturbances
emanation_groups.magnetizing: Drawing beings to the Dharma, gathering auspicious conditions
emanation_groups.wrathful: Subjugating demons, obstacles, and negative forces

key_eminations: Black Tara — wrathful subjugation of demons
Iron Hook Red Tara — drawing of good fortune
